conventional_view: Most California public-agency lawyers would conclude Okafor has a disqualifying financial interest "in" the contract under § 1090 — both through the spouse's community-property salary interest (see Family Code § 760; People v. Honig; Thomson v. Call) and independently through uncompensated board membership on a bidding nonprofit — and that no § 1091 remote-interest or § 1091.5 noninterest exception cleanly reaches a spouse's compensated employment with the bidder. Because § 1090's prohibition and voidness remedy attach to the whole governmental body/contract rather than being cured by one member's abstention (Stigall v. City of Taft; Thomson v. Call), recusal alone does not save an award to Harbor Light; the conventional advice is that Okafor must resign the board seat and the spousal financial nexus to Harbor Light must be eliminated (or Harbor Light must be excluded from this particular procurement) before the city can safely contract with it, in addition to mandatory PRA § 87100/87103 recusal from both the framework and award votes.
disfavored_position: The city may lawfully contract with Harbor Light if Okafor simply recuses from both the RFP-framework vote and the award vote (with full disclosure), without resigning the board seat and without any change to the spouse's employment — on the view that the board seat and/or spousal salary interest qualifies for treatment as a remote interest under § 1091 or a noninterest under § 1091.5, that the salary is fixed and not contingent on this specific contract, and that recusal satisfies both § 1090 and the Political Reform Act's materiality and public-generally provisions in this setting.
